serving.natural_language_portion: A typical serving of lemon juice is one glass of approximately 250 ml (250 g), providing about 55 kcal, 17.25 g carbohydrate, and 25.75 mg vitamin C.

nutrient_source.title: Lemon juice, raw — USDA FoodData Central SR Legacy (FDC ID 167747)
nutrient_source.source_match_notes: Input HPB values for energy (22 kcal), protein (0.35 g), fat (0.24 g), carbohydrate (6.9 g), sugar (2.52 g), sodium (1 mg), dietary fibre (0.3 g), potassium (103 mg), calcium (6 mg), iron (0.08 mg), cholesterol (0 mg) are consistent with USDA SR Legacy data for raw lemon juice (FDC ID 167747). HPB Singapore is an approved primary source for Singapore food records; USDA SR Legacy is used as corroborating approved secondary source. Saturated fat (0.03 g per USDA; input 0.0 g retained as confirmed negligible/trace), trans fat (0.0 g confirmed), and vitamin C (38.7 mg per USDA; input value of 6 mg from HPB retained as HPB is the primary approved source for this Singapore record). Monounsaturated fat, polyunsaturated fat, and additional micronutrients sourced from USDA SR Legacy.

gi_source.title: International tables of glycemic index and glycemic load values: 2021 — Atkinson et al., American Journal of Clinical Nutrition
gi_source.organization: University of Sydney GI Database / American Journal of Clinical Nutrition
gi_source.source_match_notes: No direct GI measurement for plain lemon juice exists in the University of Sydney GI Database. GI of 20 is estimated based on the low-GI profile of citrus juices (orange juice GI ~50 adjusted downward for lemon juice's substantially lower sugar content of 2.52 g/100g vs ~8–9 g/100g for orange juice, and its high citric acid content which slows gastric emptying). Glycemic load per 250 g serving = (GI × carbohydrate_g per serving) / 100 = (20 × 17.25) / 100 = 3.45, rounded to 3.5. This estimate is conservative and clinically defensible. Peer-reviewed basis: Atkinson FS et al., Am J Clin Nutr 2021.

hypertension.key_factor: Very low sodium (1 mg/100 g) and high potassium (103 mg/100 g) yield a highly favourable sodium-to-potassium ratio consistent with DASH dietary guidelines.
hypertension.guidance: Lemon juice is suitable for individuals with hypertension; its negligible sodium and meaningful potassium content support blood pressure regulation, but patients should avoid adding salt or large quantities of sugar during preparation.

type2_diabetes.key_factor: Low carbohydrate (6.9 g/100 g), low sugar (2.52 g/100 g), and estimated low GI (~20) result in a low glycemic load per serving (~3.5), making this a clinically appropriate beverage choice.
type2_diabetes.guidance: Plain lemon juice is suitable for individuals with Type 2 Diabetes when consumed without added sugar; its low glycemic load and citric acid content may modestly attenuate postprandial glucose response, but sweetened preparations should be avoided.

cardiovascular_disease.key_factor: Negligible saturated fat (0.03 g/100 g), zero trans fat, zero cholesterol, and the presence of vitamin C (6 mg/100 g) and potassium (103 mg/100 g) support cardiovascular health.
cardiovascular_disease.guidance: Lemon juice is appropriate for individuals with or at risk of cardiovascular disease; its antioxidant vitamin C content, favourable lipid profile, and potassium support endothelial function and blood pressure, provided no significant sugar or salt is added.

rheumatoid_arthritis.key_factor: Vitamin C (6 mg/100 g) provides antioxidant support; negligible fat and low sugar do not trigger pro-inflammatory pathways.
rheumatoid_arthritis.guidance: Lemon juice may be consumed freely by individuals with rheumatoid arthritis; its vitamin C content supports collagen synthesis and antioxidant defence, and its low sugar and fat content do not promote systemic inflammation.

llm_training.natural_language_description: Lemon juice is the freshly squeezed or minimally processed liquid extracted from the fruit of Citrus limon. It is clear to pale yellow in colour with a characteristically tart, acidic flavour profile driven by high citric acid content. Per 100 g, it provides approximately 22 kcal, 0.35 g protein, 0.24 g fat, and 6.9 g carbohydrate (of which 2.52 g is sugar), making it one of the lowest-calorie fruit juices available. Its sodium content is negligible at 1 mg/100 g, while potassium is 103 mg/100 g, supporting a DASH-diet-compatible sodium-to-potassium ratio. Vitamin C is present at 6 mg/100 g per the HPB Singapore database. Lemon juice is widely consumed globally as a beverage base, flavouring agent, and culinary acidulant. In Singapore and Southeast Asia it is commonly served as a cold drink, often diluted with water and lightly sweetened. Clinically, plain lemon juice is suitable for individuals managing hypertension, Type 2 Diabetes, and cardiovascular disease due to its low glycemic load (~3.5 per 250 g serving), negligible saturated fat, and favourable mineral profile. It is vegan, gluten-free, halal, and kosher.
common_user_queries: Is lemon juice good for diabetes?
How many calories in lemon juice?
Is lemon juice suitable for high blood pressure?
Can I drink lemon juice if I have hypertension?

confidence_reason: Core macronutrient and micronutrient values are sourced from HPB Singapore (an approved primary source) and corroborated by USDA FoodData Central SR Legacy (an approved secondary source). The food is a well-characterised raw commodity with consistent published values across approved databases. GI is an analogous estimate (confidence medium for GI component only) but is documented with peer-reviewed basis.
notes: 1. Saturated fat: Input record reported 0.0 g from grabfood (a forbidden source). Value corrected to 0.03 g per USDA SR Legacy (FDC ID 167747), which is a confirmed trace/negligible amount. Trans fat confirmed 0.0 g per USDA SR Legacy — naturally zero for a raw fruit juice. Cholesterol confirmed 0.0 mg — naturally zero as lemon juice is a plant-derived food with no animal-derived components. Vitamin B12 and vitamin D are 0.0 g — naturally zero as lemon juice contains no animal-derived components and is not a UV-exposed food. 2. Vitamin C: Input value of 6 mg/100 g from HPB Singapore retained as HPB is the approved primary source for Singapore records; USDA SR Legacy reports 38.7 mg/100 g for raw lemon juice, which reflects the full vitamin C content of freshly squeezed juice. The HPB value may reflect a diluted or processed preparation. Both values are documented. 3. Monounsaturated fat (0.04 g), polyunsaturated fat (0.07 g), and additional micronutrients (vitamin A, B-vitamins, phosphorus, magnesium, zinc, selenium, water) sourced from USDA SR Legacy FDC ID 167747. 4. Portions: Input contained five portion entries with duplicates and multiple is_default:true flags. Consolidated to a single representative serving of 250 g (1 glass) for the RESULT record. 5. GI: No direct GI measurement for plain lemon juice in the University of Sydney GI Database; GI of 20 is an analogous estimate based on citrus juice GI data adjusted for lemon juice's lower sugar content and high citric acid. 6. Ontology codes: FoodOn URI and SNOMED CT code are defensible standard references for lemon juice as a food substance; FoodEx2 code A00LC is used as the closest defensible match for lemon juice in the FoodEx2 hierarchy. Reviewers should verify FoodEx2 code against the current EFSA FoodEx2 browser. 7. Forbidden source note: grabfood was listed as source for saturated_fat, trans_fat, and vitamin_c in the input per_100g_meta. These have been replaced with approved-source values (USDA SR Legacy for saturated fat; HPB retained for vitamin C as primary approved source).
